excerpt_en: So today was a full work day! It was a day with no plans to meet anyone. Days like these are precious time where I can focus and dive deep into work. It's the perfect timing to knock out all those things I tend to keep putting off.
body_en:
So today was a full work day!

It was a day with no plans to meet anyone.

Days like these are

precious time where I can focus and dive deep into work.

It's the perfect timing to knock out all those things I tend to keep putting off.

Today's main task was making reservations.

Specifically, booking flights.

Now that my schedule is mostly set, if I keep putting it off, tickets could skyrocket in price at the last minute, or the flights I want could fill up.

Also, accommodation and local plans largely depend on having the flights booked first, so it was important to get this sorted out.

March Itinerary Confirmed!

After organizing my schedule through late March, I decided to go to Dubai and Paris.

These two cities are absolutely essential locations when thinking about the future direction of FOMUS.

In order to present FOMUS masu not just as traditional crafts but as luxury art pieces to the world, I first need to gauge the response in cities with high-end markets.

From that perspective,

Dubai and Paris are the perfect targets.

Dubai: Taking on the Luxury Market

Dubai is a city where the world's wealthy gather, and it's characterized by an extremely well-developed luxury goods market.

Traditional crafts are highly valued there, and there's a deeply rooted culture of cherishing "rarity" and "exclusivity."

To brand FOMUS masu not as mere vessels but as premium items, it's essential to understand how they would be received in such a market.

Especially in Dubai, where gift-giving culture thrives, I'm also considering developing them as "special gifts" for the wealthy.

Also, after April, temperatures soar and it becomes brutally hot just walking outside, which limits activities.

That's why March, when it's just barely not too hot, is the best time to visit.

Furthermore, March overlaps with the Ramadan period.

In Islamic countries, people fast during Ramadan, so daytime activities are more subdued, but nightlife culture becomes extremely vibrant after sunset.

I believe that by experiencing the local lifestyle and culture during this special period, it will be an opportunity to explore how FOMUS masu can hold value.

For example, in Islamic culture, there are unique preferences when it comes to choosing tableware and vessels.

From this perspective, I want to research how Japanese masu might be received by actually talking with local people.

Paris: To the Center of Art and Fashion

Meanwhile, Paris also holds great potential as an important base for FOMUS.

That's because France is a country with strong interest in both traditional crafts and anime culture.

Particularly, since my visit to Paris will coincide with Paris Fashion Week, it's a perfect chance to receive new inspiration in the fields of fashion and art.

When considering the possibility of developing masu as art pieces or accessories, I can explore how they might merge with the world of high-end fashion brands.

Also, since I'm considering Paris as a potential base for activities from fall 2025 onward, this trip also includes a scouting element.

When considering a long-term stay, it's very important to check livability and business-friendliness in advance.

Furthermore, Paris has many people interested in Japanese culture, and traditional Japanese crafts are highly regarded there.

Therefore, I believe it will be an excellent test market for exploring how FOMUS masu can be developed not just as practical items but as art pieces or interior items.

The Potential of the French Market

French people tend to embrace Japanese traditional culture as "art," and many find value in uniquely Japanese aesthetics like tea ceremony and calligraphy.

FOMUS masu should also be able to gain higher value by positioning them not just as "vessels for drinks" but as part of art and lifestyle.

Also, collaborations with French galleries and art events are possible, so one of my goals during this stay is to contact local art professionals and expand my network.

A Journey to Pioneer FOMUS's Future

This Dubai-Paris trip is also

not just sightseeing, but

a critical mission that will

greatly shape FOMUS's future.

  • In Dubai, explore the possibility of entering the luxury market
  • In Paris, strengthen FOMUS branding from art and fashion perspectives

I'm convinced that the inspiration and connections gained in these two cities will be major hints for FOMUS's next stage.

Through on-the-ground experiences, I'll be charting a path for FOMUS masu to evolve beyond the framework of "Japanese traditional crafts" into art pieces loved around the world.

I'm already excited to see what discoveries await on this journey.

title_en: Walking Events Are Full of Benefits
excerpt_en: So since it was Saturday, I held a walking event for the first time in a while. I've been moving around overseas a lot lately, so the frequency of events had dropped a bit, but in-person interactions are still fun, and I once again felt it's an important activity for expanding FOMUS's possibilities.
body_en:
So since it was Saturday,

I held a walking event for the first time in a while.

I've been moving around overseas a lot lately, so the frequency of events had dropped a bit,

but in-person interactions are still fun, and I once again felt it's an important activity for expanding FOMUS's possibilities.

🔻Walking Event as an International Exchange Community

Just to reiterate,

I've created an international exchange

community in Ireland.

Within that,

I organize walking events,

and about 5 to 20 people participate.

The purpose of the events is

to get people to know MaSU,

and as a result, to get them to know FOMUS.

Additionally, it's also about improving my own English skills

and building a network with people overseas.

And

this has great significance for me personally:

  • Improving English skills
  • Building a network with people overseas
  • Deepening multicultural understanding

These are things I can

achieve simultaneously through these events.

Also, this was unexpected, but

apparently I'm the only Japanese organizer running events like this in Dublin, so the differentiation of my activities is solidly established.

So anyway,

this time 7 people participated.

Two were repeat attendees,

and five were newcomers.

Since my community uses the "Meetup" app, it's not just acquaintances gathering -- a lot of first-timers come each time, which is a key feature.

That's exactly why I get to meet people with all kinds of backgrounds, and different conversations and interactions emerge every time, making it fun.

Participants often tell me, "There's no other exchange event like this."

In particular, it's not just walking -- I've created a system where everyone gets to talk to each other, so conversations flow naturally, and people become friends in a relaxed atmosphere.

As a result, it's become a very popular event.

An Established Community Format

It's been about 5 months since I started this walking event.

Although I started from scratch, now there are new participants every time, and the number of regulars has been growing.

Since coming to Ireland, being able to create my own community and build a system for connecting with people through it is one of my biggest achievements, I think.

Also, the surveys and reviews from each event have been extremely positive, so I feel the satisfaction level is high.

Going forward, my time in Ireland will decrease, but whenever I'm back, I'd like to hold some kind of event again.

🔻Refresh DAY

After the walking event,

I had plans to go on an outing with a Japanese friend I'd gotten close to here.

We were planning to go to Malahide, about 30 minutes by train from Dublin city center, but

for some reason,

the train to Malahide was suspended and we couldn't go...

So we changed our destination at the station.

We decided to go to Bray, a place I'd run through once during my Medoc Marathon training.

It was supposed to be just the two of us, but

by chance, her housemate was

on the same train,

and since she didn't have any particular plans either,

she ended up joining us.

First, after arriving in Bray,

we had lunch.

I hadn't eaten anything since lunchtime the day before,

so I was absolutely starving.

Since there were three of us this time,

we could share various dishes.

Then, after a quick stroll around Bray,

the rain started coming down hard,

so we headed back to Dublin

and went out for drinks.

My friend and I

are the same age,

so we feel a strong sense of kinship.

But our backgrounds are completely different,

and we're from different places.

Yet by chance, we happened to be

in Ireland at the same time,

and actually, I first met her

at one of the walking events I organize.

Everything comes down to timing,

so I want to cherish encounters like this.

We ended up spending about 2-3 hours at a pub, drinking

and chatting about work, personal life,

and all sorts of things.

Irish Pub Culture and the Importance of Refreshing

When you think of Ireland, you think of pub culture.

It's really great that there are spaces where you can casually invite someone for drinks and everyone can relax and enjoy.

Thanks to having places for this kind of casual interaction, friendships seem to naturally expand.

Also, since I usually focus on work and projects, I felt it's important to consciously make time to refresh like this.

I feel like making time for this once or twice a month helps organize my thoughts and generate new ideas.

So both the morning and afternoon turned out to be a great time!

excerpt_en: I headed to Cork to visit the distillery of Jameson Whiskey, famous for Irish whiskey. Cork is known as Ireland's second city, and you can get there from Dublin in about 3 hours by express train. It's kind of like going from Tokyo to Osaka.
body_en:
I headed to Cork to visit the distillery of Jameson Whiskey, famous for Irish whiskey.

Cork is known as Ireland's second city, and you can get there from Dublin in about 3 hours by express train.

It's kind of like going from Tokyo to Osaka.

For me, since I'm used to expeditions that cross entire countries, being able to go by train is refreshing in a way.

Still, from the hotel where I'm staying,

the factory where Jameson Whiskey is made

is about 4 hours one way.

The factory isn't in Cork itself but in Midleton,

which is about another 30 minutes

by public transportation from Cork.

So I headed out first thing in the morning.

This is what my seat looked like.

I safely arrived at the Midleton factory

and got to take the tour.

During my Portugal trip, the wine tour

was easy to understand in English,

but the Irish English

was very hard to catch,

and honestly, I couldn't understand what they were saying.

I was painfully reminded that my English still has a long way to go.

There's also a Jameson tour available in Dublin,

but that one is no longer operational

and has been turned into a tourist shop.

The difference with Midleton is that

it's actually still in operation.

So you can see

the actual equipment being used, which is the main feature.

However,

as for the tasting,

it was pretty standard.

By the way, the tasting experience at the tourist-oriented tour in Dublin was...

This is the tasting area. Lol.

The level is completely different.

They put their effort into different things, huh.

Having visited both,

I was able to understand that each serves a different role.

🔻On to Cork

After leaving the Midleton distillery,

I headed to Cork city center.

My Cork stay was one night, two days.

It was necessary to see how it differs from Dublin,

so I checked out souvenir shops and tourist spots.

Honestly,

it felt like a smaller version of Dublin.

You know how Tokyo and Osaka have different cultures?

But Dublin and Cork --

the atmosphere of the cities isn't that different.

Maybe other countries aren't that different within themselves?

In Japan, traveling around gives you different vibes which is fun,

but with Dublin and Cork in Ireland,

really just the scale is different.

Plus, it's such a small city that

you can easily walk around the whole thing.

I went to Elizabeth Fort, which you can enter for free,

and looked out over the town, but since it was cloudy with occasional rain,

it wasn't exactly a breathtaking spot. Lol.

After that, I continued sightseeing around the city.

Cork city center is so small that

one night, two days seems just right, don't you think?

That's what I thought.

Tomorrow I'll do a little more sightseeing and then head back to Dublin.

title_en: Exploring Cork and On to the Next Journey
excerpt_en: So it was day 2 of my Cork stay. By yesterday, I'd already gotten a good sense of the size and vibe of Cork's streets, so today I decided to walk around looking for places that are more uniquely Cork. English Market: First, I headed to...
body_en:
So it was day 2 of my Cork stay.

By yesterday, I'd already gotten a good sense of the size and vibe of Cork's streets,

so today I decided to walk around looking for "places that are uniquely Cork."

English Market

First, I headed to one of Cork's famous markets, the English Market.

It's a place where local produce gathers -- meat, fish, spices, fruits, and all sorts of food are sold here.

The market was lively, and what was impressive was seeing not just tourists but locals doing their regular shopping too.

However, looking at the actual prices, everything was generally more expensive compared to Portugal.

Especially for fruits and seafood, the markets in Portugal felt more reasonably priced.

True to an Irish market, there was an abundance of sausages, bacon, and cheese varieties, and the meat products all looked delicious.

Since I'm not actually living here, I can't fully grasp the everyday cost of living,

but I reconfirmed the impression that food costs in Ireland are generally high.

To Cork University

After the market, I headed to one of Cork's sightseeing spots, University College Cork (UCC).

This university is open to anyone, so I took the opportunity to walk around inside.

There were many historic buildings, and the entire campus was very beautiful.

The stone school buildings and sprawling grassy courtyards were particularly impressive, with a calm atmosphere throughout.

Since it was lunchtime, I decided to have lunch at the university cafeteria.

Today's lunch was a sandwich (about 5 euros).

They also had Irish dishes and soups among other options.

By around noon, the cafeteria was nearly full.

It was bustling with students, and the age range was, as expected, mostly young people.

(* Totally judging by appearances here.)

I'd heard that Japanese students attend here too, but unfortunately I didn't run into any today.

Still, it was nice to get a feel for the campus atmosphere.

I'm not taking classes or anything, but it's interesting to catch glimpses of local student life like this.

After lunch, I went back to exploring the city.

However, since I'd already hit the main tourist spots yesterday, I found myself in a state where I honestly had nothing left to do.

The city was smaller than I expected, and I felt like I'd pretty much seen everything in two days.

So I decided to head back to the hotel early and get some work done.

When you travel like this, you inevitably end up moving around a lot, making it hard to secure solid blocks of time.

That's exactly why it's important to have days where you go all-in on work when you have the time.

I was able to get several hours of solid work done at the hotel, so it turned out to be time well spent.

In the afternoon, I took the 3:25 PM express train from Cork back to Dublin.

Irish trains are comfortable, and the roughly 2.5-hour journey wasn't too much of a burden -- I spent it working on my laptop.

After arriving in Dublin, I immediately checked into my hotel and switched back to work mode.

I got started on preparations for the Nordic expedition starting tomorrow.

Starting tomorrow, the Nordic expedition begins!

The first destination is Copenhagen, Denmark.

It's been a while since I was last in Copenhagen, so I'm looking forward to seeing what's changed.

In particular, Nordic design culture pairs well with FOMUS's art and craft direction, so I want to treasure the inspiration I find there.

Also, since Denmark is a country with high sustainability awareness, I'm planning to research the potential for environmentally conscious masu as well.

So with that, my Cork stay is over.

Next, I'm heading to the Nordics!

excerpt_en: So today the Nordic expedition begins. First up is Denmark. Copenhagen. So I headed to Dublin Airport a bit early. The reason, of course, is to use the lounge. I have a Priority Pass, so...
body_en:
So today the Nordic expedition begins.

First up is Denmark🇩🇰

Copenhagen.

So,

I headed to Dublin Airport

a bit early.

The reason, of course,

is to use the lounge.

I have a Priority Pass,

so I can use airport lounges all over the world.

Thanks to this,

I've developed a habit of getting to the airport early,

which means I'm less likely to miss my flights. Lol.

Anyway,

I took a 1 PM flight to Copenhagen.

🔻To Copenhagen

I arrived in Copenhagen for the first time in about 8 years and 5 months.

The last time I visited was during my university days,

when I came to Denmark for a seminar study trip.

Back then, I was serving as the seminar leader,

and I was planning and running

the activities on-site as the leader.

Through my experience as seminar leader in university and this overseas study trip,

I learned about community management and leadership, but...

Who would have thought that 8 years later,

I'd be back in Denmark for work!

You never know what life has in store, huh. Lol.

🔻Why I Came to Copenhagen

On this visit,

I'll be pursuing multiple goals simultaneously.

1. Learning Nordic Art & Design Culture

Denmark is the center of Nordic design.

The philosophy of simple, functional design and sustainable craftsmanship is deeply rooted here.

In particular, the fusion of Japanese traditional crafts and Nordic design is a field with new possibilities for FOMUS, so I'll learn on-site and explore how it can be applied.

2. Exploring Collaboration Possibilities with Danish Companies

I've already reached out to a five-star hotel in Copenhagen,

proposing things like "Could FOMUS masu be handled as a luxury product?"

I haven't received a reply yet, but I believe that continuing these kinds of approaches will lead to future opportunities.

The key is to "just take action."

3. Scouting for FOMUS Masu Photo Locations (Shooting Plans for Summer Onward)

I also have plans to shoot FOMUS Masu Photos in the Nordics in the future, so this trip doubles as location scouting.

I plan to walk around and research which areas of Copenhagen are suitable for shoots.

4. Connecting with Local People

Since I'm in the Nordics, I want to interact with as many local people as possible.

So I tried looking for Danish communities on Facebook groups, but...

Surprisingly, there seem to be few groups for "international exchange" or "business networking".

Still, I've submitted join requests to a few groups, so I'll see whether I can build a local network.

5. Market Research for "MASUKAME (tentative)," Planned to Launch Spring 2025 Onward

As FOMUS's next development, I'm planning to launch a souvenir brand called "MASUKAME (tentative)".

This will be a product line based on Japanese traditional crafts but with enhanced branding to make it easier to sell overseas.

I'll visit local variety shops and galleries in the Danish market to research whether this "craft x design" approach would be accepted.

🔻Maximizing Results in a Short Time

This Copenhagen stay is

limited to 6 days,

so I don't expect everything to go perfectly in a single visit.

Still, if I can find even a little "spark" or "seed of possibility," that should connect to future developments.

Also, since my activities in Europe will increase going forward, it's important to build connections with the Nordic region including Denmark.

To that end, I'll meet as many people as possible, gather information, and determine my next steps during this stay.

🔻Learning Nordic Design

Denmark's design culture shares something with Japan's "wabi-sabi" and "minimalism," and I feel they're quite compatible.

For example,

  • Nordic-style masu utilizing Japanese woodworking techniques
  • Object masu collaborating with Danish furniture design
  • Nordic sauna culture x masu drink experience

I want to see how these ideas are perceived from a local perspective.

🔻First, Take Action. Then, Connect It to the Next Step

I've done what preparation I could before coming to Denmark, but ultimately, it all comes down to taking action on the ground.

Especially when pioneering new territory overseas, nothing progresses by "just waiting," so it's crucial to take the initiative and move proactively.

Over the next 6 days, I'll be enjoying Copenhagen to the fullest while exploring business possibilities!

Stay tuned for the next report!

title_en: Learning at the Home of Design
excerpt_en: So today my Copenhagen activities officially kick off. I'm staying in the city center, and most of the tourist spots are within walking distance. First, I headed to the Design Museum right when it opened. It cost about 2,500 yen to enter...
body_en:
So today my Copenhagen activities

officially kick off.

I'm staying in the city center, and most of the tourist spots are within walking distance.

First, I headed to

the Design Museum right when it opened.

It cost about 2,500 yen to enter.

The benefit of going first thing in the morning is that there aren't many visitors yet.

I'd heard it gets crowded even on weekdays, so I made sure to go early.

And what immediately surprised me was that

the very first design exhibit was Japanese ukiyo-e!

I was surprised to see Japanese design right off the bat at a Copenhagen museum.

And there was a whole bunch of stuff on caricatures, war history,

and the Yoshiwara pleasure district.

From there, it moved on to Danish design.

Various arts and designs were lined up in rows.

Personally, I had two purposes:

one was to apply what I learn to the gallery planned to open on Sagishima Island in April 2025,

and the other was to build up my own design and art

knowledge and education.

Nearby, a group of elementary school-aged kids

were sketching the designs on display.

Perhaps there's a culture in Copenhagen

of exposing children to art from a young age.

The education must be completely different from Japan's.

And there was yet another piece of Japanese culture turned into art.

It was the "tsuba" -- the sword guard.

It's the piece placed between the blade and the handle of a katana.

Since I practiced kendo,

the tsuba is something very familiar to me,

but I never expected to see it as art like this...

Do they even have something like this in Japan? Lol.

I never expected to see

tsuba art in Denmark of all places. Lol.

For FOMUS too,

it would be amazing if someday

masu art could be displayed in a place like this.

By the way, the souvenir section also had a Japan booth.

Ukiyo-e plates and illustrations were on display.

I'd love to get FOMUS in there too! Lol.

By carving ukiyo-e onto masu,

we could cater to the audience's taste,

but that wouldn't be very interesting,

so I want to think more about how to make them want to display it.

🔻To Kastellet Fortress

After the Design Museum, I headed to the fortress.

This time, I don't have plans to do

Masu Photo shoots with local people,

but I'm planning to come back to Copenhagen

after May this year

to do shoots then.

Since it's winter now and fewer people want to participate in shoots,

I'll do a big batch of shooting when it gets a bit warmer.

So I went to the fortress as a scouting trip.

This was a really photogenic spot.

When you just take landscape photos with an iPhone,

you can't quite tell,

but as a photographer

using a DSLR, this place is definitely a go.

By the way, this fortress,

like Goryokaku in Hokkaido,

is star-shaped.

I wonder if a pentagonal shape

really is the most efficient for defense?

🔻To HAY House

HAY House is a shop and gallery in Copenhagen, Denmark, themed around contemporary design.

HAY is one of Denmark's leading design brands, and its concept store, HAY House, is a popular place where you can experience the brand's philosophy and design firsthand.

There were tons of stylishly designed items,

and it was incredibly interesting.

I also discovered things like "Oh, you can do it this way too."

These are chopsticks,

but they've added color and made the packaging stylish.

However, they were made in China.

For FOMUS,

it's totally possible to add color to masu and sell them,

and the question becomes how to make the packaging work as a souvenir.

The masu itself is a top-tier product as content, so it's all about how to sell it globally,

and one key aspect is packaging, I thought.

Right now, FOMUS doesn't even have

masu packaging yet,

so I strongly felt that packaging that makes people want to buy it as a souvenir or gift is absolutely essential.

Also, when launching the new brand "Masu-Game," I'm planning to vary the designs by region, so I felt that packaging design that makes people want to collect them all is necessary too.

From FOMUS's perspective,

as a souvenir brand:

  • Design packaging for individual masu
  • Design packaging that makes people want to collect design masu

From a packaging design perspective, it's the same concept,

but since each serves a different role,

I felt the need to create them properly.

Being able to think about things like this

is precisely because I went around visiting design-related places,

and it made coming to Copenhagen worthwhile.

By the way, what do you think this photo is?

These are matches.

Even ordinary matches, with just one design touch,

become a stylish item and even interior decor.

The point is, with just one idea,

you can change the meaning of anything and make it sellable.

🔻I Need to Learn More About Design and Art

I also visited several other design-related shops.

My design knowledge up to now

has really only been a fraction,

and I felt I need to see much more of the world and build up my knowledge and education in design and art.

The FOMUS brand too

is all about combining different things.

In other words, to go further,

I myself need to learn more,

and beyond that, deeper connections with designers will naturally emerge.

If you know nothing,

creatives won't come to you,

so a certain level of knowledge is necessary.

Today was a day where I deeply understood

that I'm still lacking in that area too.

In the evening, I passed by the entrance of

Tivoli Gardens, which I also visited 8 years ago,

and had drinks at the bar next to my accommodation.

Alright, I'll keep at it again tomorrow!

excerpt_en: 1/30 & 1/31. My activities might look flashy, but in reality, there's a lot of painstaking work involved. I've been surveying the streets of Copenhagen during breaks between online work and at lunchtime. However, on this day I barely went out and focused on the future development of FOMUS Parure.
body_en:
1/30 & 1/31

My activities might look flashy, but in reality, there's a lot of painstaking work involved.

I've been surveying the streets of Copenhagen during breaks between online work and at lunchtime.

However, on this day I barely went out and focused on the future development of FOMUS Parure.

In order to bring FOMUS Parure to more people overseas, I decided to completely shift away from the Japanese market

and aim to establish it as a luxury brand specializing in overseas markets.

Along with that, I'm significantly revising the business model and pricing, and working on restructuring the brand.

There's a mountain of things to do, from website renovations to rethinking sales strategies,

but I want to have everything ready

by the time I visit Dubai and Paris in March.

🔻Copenhagen Lunch and Shopping Road

I headed out for lunch while strolling through the city.

I went to a popular buffet restaurant with several locations within Denmark, and was lucky enough to use a January weekday-only coupon, so I managed to eat for about 2,000 yen, which is a steal considering Copenhagen's prices.

By the way, even a fast food combo meal costs about the same, so this price felt like quite a bargain.

After that, I explored the city.

I took a thorough walk through "Stroget," one of the longest shopping streets in Northern Europe.

At a store that prints tote bags,

they were selling each one for about 4,000 yen,

and my reaction was, "For THIS?!"

In Japan, it'd be about 1,000 yen.

I thought it'd be interesting to replace this concept

with masu and sell them.

👆The glass display cases in the souvenir section are a perfect fit for small masu.

I also browsed various other shops selling miscellaneous goods,

Danish variety shops carry many products with refined designs, and they were extremely helpful in thinking about how Japanese traditional craft masu could be incorporated.

In fact, Danish variety shops have many wooden mini objects and storage items lined up, and adding masu to the mix might help discover new appeal.

Also, Danish design culture is characterized by being "simple and functional while combining beauty."

Masu also has a simple form yet is packed with Japanese craftsmanship. I felt that fusing it with Nordic interior design could create new value.

Inspiration for the Sagishima Art Gallery

Walking through Copenhagen, I noticed there are many stylish exhibitions and shops, each with its own unique concept.

This city has a deeply rooted culture of valuing design, and there's a lot to learn even just from how things are displayed.

In April, I'm planning to open an art gallery on Sagishima Island, so I want to apply the inspiration I've gained from this trip to the gallery as well.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU. The "European Activity Journal" is now on its third installment. Here, MaSU writes about the behind-the-scenes stories of overseas expansion for the masu brand "FOMUS" -- the stuff that can't be written publicly -- along with life overseas.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU.

The "European Activity Journal" is now on its third installment.

Here, MaSU writes about

the behind-the-scenes stories of overseas expansion

for the masu brand "FOMUS" --

the stuff that can't be written publicly --

along with life overseas, all recorded through my own firsthand experiences.

It's partly a record for myself,

but I've also made these activity records available as a subscription,

so subscribers can

vicariously experience my activities,

and in the future, when I've made it big,

these articles will document the daily struggles when things weren't going well yet,

and I also think it'd be cool if they could someday become material for a book.

Currently, I'm flying around overseas, mainly in Europe, and there aren't really any other Japanese people doing the same kind of activities as me,

so the experiences and knowledge gained there

are an "asset" for me,

and by properly recording them in writing,

they become a "visible asset" as well.

I don't know at what point

you might be reading these articles,

but I hope they can serve as some kind of reference.

excerpt_en: So we've entered February 2025. Honestly, I'm surprised that one month of this year has already gone by. Lol. In January, I gave up my place in Dublin, went on the Portugal expedition, participated in an Irish Embassy event, and...
body_en:
So we've entered February 2025.

Honestly, I'm surprised that

one month of this year has already gone by. Lol.

In January, I gave up my place in Dublin,

went on the Portugal expedition,

participated in an Irish Embassy event, and pushed my activities forward,

but from late January, the Nordic expedition started,

and I'm currently in Copenhagen, Denmark.

It's already day 5 in Copenhagen.

If you'd like to read previous articles,

please subscribe from the page below.

https://fomus.official.ec/p/00015

So in the afternoon, I walked a bit

to the other side of Copenhagen harbor.

Until now, I'd been exploring Copenhagen's tourist areas,

but this time,

since I had plans to go to the opera house in the evening,

I headed to the other side of the harbor.

I'd heard there were some

good bakeries over there,

so I figured I should try them while I had the chance

and walked about 30 minutes.

Denmark has high prices,

and food and transportation all cost money,

so I try to walk as much as possible.

First, I went to a famous bakery.

Honestly, it was too crowded,

and all the sweet pastries were sold out,

so I ended up ordering some rye bread I didn't really know.

Both the first and second floors were full, so I couldn't get a seat...

I ended up getting it to go.

This was 750 yen,

so I think that conveys how high the prices are. Lol.

I found out there was

another cafe nearby,

so I decided to check it out.

It was a cafe called HART,

and apparently they have several locations.

Actually, I'd been asking for Denmark recommendations on the Threads app,

and got several responses, among which was a message saying,

"You should definitely try the Fastelavnsbolle -- you can only get them right now!"

Fastelavnsbolle is a traditional pastry eaten in Scandinavian countries like Denmark and Norway during the carnival (Fastelavn) season.

I didn't even know about it.

And this cafe had them!

Of course I bought one!

I was amazed that this was over 1,000 yen. Lol.

Apparently, the dough varies by country:

Variations:

  • Danish style: Often made with Danish pastry dough filled with custard cream or marzipan.
  • Norwegian style: Brioche-like bread dough sandwiched with whipped cream and jam.
  • Swedish style (Semla): A similar treat -- in Sweden, they eat "Semla," which are buns filled with almond paste and whipped cream.

So,

the one I ate was the Danish version with Danish pastry dough.

Well, of course it would be -- I'm in Denmark after all. Lol.

Since I'll be visiting Sweden and Norway after this, I might be able to complete the full Nordic set.

The taste was exactly as it looked,

and it was incredibly delicious!

I really wanted to eat two,

but they were just too expensive, so I held back.

🔻To the Opera

In the evening, I went to the Danish Opera House.

It was my first opera ever,

and my goal was simply,

"I want to experience what opera is like."

So I didn't care about the program or the seats.

A few days before, I searched for tickets

and bought the second cheapest ticket (the cheapest being standing).

And I actually went and watched it.

What I saw this time was an Italian production --

the opera "Maria Stuarda."

Detailed Story of the Opera "Maria Stuarda"

"Maria Stuarda" is a historical opera depicting the political and personal conflict between Mary, Queen of Scots (Maria Stuarda) and Queen Elizabeth I of England (Elisabetta).

I went in having lightly read up on the story beforehand, but the opera itself was in Italian,

and the subtitles were in Danish,

so honestly, I had absolutely

no idea what they were saying.

Also, unlike musicals,

all the dialogue is sung in opera, so it's all high notes.

So when you can't understand the language,

it's just someone hitting high notes the whole time. Lol.

On top of that,

I thought it would be about an hour long,

but it kept going and going,

so I looked it up during intermission,

and the performance time for the opera "Maria Stuarda" is about 2 hours 30 minutes to 3 hours (including intermission, about 3 hours to 3 hours 30 minutes).

Apparently. Lol.

It's ridiculously long. Lol.

The story ends with one woman being executed, and in my heart, I wanted it to end so badly that I was thinking, "Just get executed already."

As an opera experience, it was terrible. Lol.

If I'm ever going to watch one again, I'd want to properly see an opera in Japan first, I thought.

Content aside,

having come to the opera for the first time,

what personally surprised me was the audience.

Everyone was elderly.

There were hardly any young people.

The performers included about 30 people on stage,

plus about 30 in the orchestra.

So there were over 60 performers,

and it felt like these wealthy-looking elderly folks were paying decent money and keeping this business running.

Also, before the show and during intermission, people were drinking wine and socializing, and these kinds of face-to-face gatherings might be important for people who live locally.

Of course, I didn't see any Japanese people,

and nobody else was there alone as a guy. Lol.

What I learned from this is:

social venues like this

attract a certain caliber of people,

you should experience things in a language you understand,

and going with someone is infinitely more fun.

Same with today's cafe --

eating sweets alone is tasty enough,

but I can't help feeling that the real value lies in being able to share it with someone,

so when eating good food overseas,

I really felt that it's better to be with someone.

It was a day full of learning!

excerpt_en: So it's day 6 of my Copenhagen activities. Since I'm departing tomorrow evening, this is essentially the last day. The struggle of Denmark life: My accommodation is in the city center, but since there's no kitchen, I basically have to eat out. Denmark's prices are double, so...
body_en:
So it's day 6 of my Copenhagen activities.

Since I'm departing tomorrow evening,

this is essentially the last day.

The Struggle of Denmark Life

My accommodation is in the city center,

but since there's no kitchen,

I basically have to eat out.

Denmark's prices are double,

so food costs would spiral out of control during this period.

I'd been living a life of eating at a fast food place during lunch, then getting by on nuts and chocolate for dinner.

When I'm eating with someone,

I eat good food,

but when I'm alone, I generally don't splurge,

and besides, this isn't a vacation -- it's work.

I have a sweet tooth,

but I'm not particular about regular meals.

So anyway,

I was making an effort to keep daily food costs under 2,000 yen.

But because of that, I've been living on fast food, so the health aspect is a bit concerning. Lol.

I'm still young enough to handle it for now,

but if I did the same thing a few years from now,

the cost of getting healthy again would probably be higher,

so this kind of saving is only for now. Lol.

By the way, my accommodation is only 12,000 yen for 6 nights,

so I'm staying quite cheaply.

I'll probably be coming back to Denmark

after May this year

for FOMUS Masu Photo shoots,

so next time, even if the accommodation costs a bit more,

I'll go for a place with a kitchen.

Learning things like this is also part of the joy of traveling.

🔻To Royal Copenhagen

Today, I visited the Royal Copenhagen flagship store.

Royal Copenhagen is Denmark's premier porcelain brand. Since its founding, it has produced high-quality porcelain products and is particularly known for its delicate designs and traditional hand-painting techniques.

Until now, I'd been seeing Royal Copenhagen pieces at various department stores and variety shops,

but today I went to the flagship store.

The store is located in a prime spot on Stroget.

By the way, it's surrounded by nothing but luxury brands.

It's hard to convey through photos alone,

but it was incredibly stylish,

and the display methods were superb.

FOMUS Masu and Royal Copenhagen Porcelain

What particularly impressed me about this visit was

the diversity of designs and the ingenuity of the display methods.

For example, some Royal Copenhagen pieces featured unique motifs like grasshoppers and crabs.

In Japanese porcelain, designs featuring these kinds of animals aren't that common, but applying this to FOMUS masu could be interesting.

There were also designs based on alphabet motifs, with 26 different designs developed. Taking a hint from this, I thought about creating "Alphabet Masu" for FOMUS.

For example, creating 26 types of masu from A to Z and selling them as a set could work as collector's items. Alternatively, creating masu tied to birthdays or initials and specializing them for gift purposes could be another strategy.

I also learned a lot about display methods. Royal Copenhagen didn't just arrange products on shelves -- they put thought into creating displays that told the brand's story.

This is something I can reference when displaying FOMUS masu in galleries.

Ideas like these ultimately come down to

whether you can put them into action,

and that makes a huge difference.

Even if it's not alphabets,

I want to take this inspiration

and apply it to FOMUS somehow.

The Value Created by Combining Ideas

The reason I keep traveling the world doing surveys like this is "to find hints for new ideas."

Ultimately, ideas are born from combining one thing with another.

And if you don't know those "things," you can't even generate new concepts.

That's why I believe it's important to keep flying around the world myself, learning about different cultures and designs.

By incorporating the inspiration gained through that process into FOMUS masu and the Sagishima art gallery, I should be able to create new value.
